slides.title: Sync vs async — dominoes vs branches
body: Synchronous is a single line of dominoes — hit the first, they fall in order, each depends on the one before. Pull one out and the rest stop.

Asynchronous is hitting one domino that branches into multiple parallel paths — they run independently. Like a phone call (sync) vs a text message (async): you reply when you have time, not in real time.

slides.title: The five async flavors
body: Batch (huge data sets, chunked), Queueable (chained, ordered, modern default), @future (simple fire-and-forget), Schedulable (runs on a cron).
Plus Platform Events — fire-and-forget pub/sub. Anyone can subscribe; the publisher doesn't know or care. Five flavors to fit different scenarios.

slides.title: Async runs on interfaces
body: Most async flavors are interfaces Salesforce defines: Batchable, Queueable, Schedulable. You implement the interface; Salesforce calls your methods on its schedule. Like the engine analogy from L10 — the framework doesn't care WHAT your start/stop methods do, only that they exist.
The exception is @future — a method-level annotation, not a class-level interface. Less control, fewer features.

vocabulary.def: A contract declaring method signatures without implementing them. Implementing classes promise to provide those methods.

slides.title: @future — the simplest
body: Mark a static void method @future. Calling it queues it to run later, in its own transaction with fresh limits.
Constraints: must be static, void, and parameters can only be primitive types (or List/Set/Map of primitives). No SObjects directly.

vocabulary.def: Annotation marking a static void method to run async in a separate transaction.

slides.title: A future method
slides.intro: Type this. notifyExternalSystem runs in the background after the caller finishes.
lineExplanations: Class.
@future annotation. callout=true permits HTTP callouts (default is false).
Method must be public static void.
Body runs in the background.
Method close. Class close.

slides.title: Queueable — the modern default
body: Implement the Queueable interface. Pass the Apex job to System.enqueueJob(). Bigger limits than @future, can pass complex Apex objects, and is chainable.
Real example: most new Apex async work uses Queueable. It replaces @future for almost every use case.

vocabulary.def: Apex interface. Implement execute(QueueableContext) and enqueue with System.enqueueJob().

slides.title: A queueable job
slides.intro: Type this. The class implements Queueable; execute() does the work.
lineExplanations: Class with 'implements Queueable' — the contract that lets it be enqueued.
Backing field for state.
Constructor stores the id passed in.
this.targetId — assign the parameter to the instance field.
Constructor close.
execute() is the method the platform calls when the job runs.
Body — do the actual work here.
Method and class close.

slides.title: Enqueueing the job
slides.intro: Type this. System.enqueueJob returns a job id you can track.
slides.code:
Id jobId = System.enqueueJob(new EmailJob('001D000000xyz'));
System.debug('Queued job ' + jobId);
lineExplanations: Construct an EmailJob with the target id, and enqueue it. Returns the new async job's id.
Print the id. The job runs in the background; this code keeps going.

slides.title: Batchable — for huge data sets
body: Three methods: start (define what to query), execute (process a chunk, default 200 records), finish (post-processing).
Real example: a nightly job that re-summarizes 5 million Opportunities. Batchable processes them in 200-row chunks, each with its own fresh governor limits.

vocabulary.def: Apex interface for processing very large data sets in chunks. Each chunk runs with fresh governor limits.

slides.title: Batchable — DailyRateCreationBatch
slides.intro: For every Contact in Salesforce, create a daily rate record. The kind of batch every multi-tenant SaaS runs at night — chunked so 500,000 contacts don't blow CPU or governor limits.
lineExplanations: Class declaration plus 'implements Database.Batchable<SObject>' — the contract requiring three methods (start, execute, finish). The <SObject> type parameter says the batch processes generic SObjects.
Blank.
Instance field for the SOQL query string. Public so it can be set externally; usually set via the constructor.
Blank.
Constructor that takes the query string. Lets callers reuse this batch class for different filters.
Assign the parameter to the instance field. 'this' disambiguates field from parameter.
Close the constructor.
Blank.
Required method 1 of 3: start. Returns a QueryLocator — Salesforce's cursor for paging through huge result sets (way past the 50K SOQL row limit).
Database.getQueryLocator wraps the SOQL string into a QueryLocator. Salesforce will then chunk the results.
Close start.
Blank.
Required method 2 of 3: execute. Runs once per chunk. 'scope' is the chunk — up to 200 records by default. Generic SObject list because the interface doesn't know your concrete type yet.
Cast the generic SObject list to a typed Contact list — now we can read first name, last name, account, etc. Fails at RUNTIME if scope isn't actually contacts.
Build an empty list — the new Daily_Rate__c records we'll insert at the end.
Walk every contact in the chunk.
Construct one Daily_Rate__c per contact, held in memory.
Set the lookup to the current contact.
Set today's date — this record represents 'today's rate.'
Random rate for demo. Real code would call an external pricing API.
Append to our to-insert list — no DML yet.
Close the loop.
ONE bulk DML insert per chunk. The 'false' is the all-or-none flag — partial success allowed.
Close execute.
Blank.
Required method 3 of 3: finish. Runs once after ALL chunks. Often used for completion email or to chain another batch.
Empty for now — but Salesforce still calls it.
Close the class.

slides.title: Kicking off the batch
slides.intro: Construct your batch class, hand it to Database.executeBatch with a chunk size, then move on. Salesforce takes it from there — your code is done as soon as the job is queued.
lineExplanations: Build the SOQL string — what records to process. Storing it as a variable lets the batch be reused with different filters.
Construct an instance of the batch class with the query.
Hand it to Database.executeBatch with a chunk size of 10. Returns the AsyncApexJob Id for tracking.
Print the Id — handy for monitoring in Apex Jobs setup, or aborting via System.abortJob(jobId).

slides.title: Schedulable — runs on a cron
body: Implement Schedulable. Schedule via Salesforce UI or System.schedule(). Cron runs your code at the chosen time.
Real example: a monthly bill-generation job. A nightly data integrity check. A weekly leaderboard recompute. All Schedulable.

vocabulary.def: Apex interface for jobs that run on a cron schedule.

slides.title: Cron expressions
body: Seven space-separated fields: seconds, minutes, hours, day-of-month, month, day-of-week, year (year optional). Plus special characters: * (every), ? (any/no specific value), L (last), / (every N), comma (list), dash (range).
Examples: '0 0 13 * * ?' fires at 1 PM every day. '0 0 2 ? * 6L' fires at 2 AM the last Friday of every month. '0 0 * * * ?' fires at the top of every hour.

vocabulary.def: A string describing a recurring schedule. Salesforce uses 7-field cron format passed to System.schedule().

slides.title: A schedulable job
slides.intro: Type this. Implementing Schedulable forces an execute(SchedulableContext) method, then System.schedule(name, cron, instance) registers it. The cron string is Salesforce's 7-field format — '0 0 2 * * ?' = 2:00 AM every day.
lineExplanations: Class declaration plus 'implements Schedulable' — the contract that requires the execute method below.
execute is the contract method. SchedulableContext is the framework's handle on the running job; usually unused.
Body — in real code this would call into a Batchable or do the work directly.
Close execute.
Close the class.
Blank.
Cron string — second, minute, hour, day-of-month, month, day-of-week, year (year optional). '0 0 2 * * ?' fires at 2:00 AM every day.
Register the job. Returns the CronTrigger Id; the framework calls execute() at every cron tick from here on.

slides.title: Platform Events — fire and forget
body: Publishers raise an event ('a thing happened'). Subscribers anywhere — Apex triggers, Flows, Lightning components, even external systems via CometD — listen and act. The publisher doesn't know or care who's listening.

Real example: imagine you shoot a flare into the sky. Anyone for miles can choose to react however they want. Useful when something happens that isn't a database transaction (a screen click, an email send, an integration ping) but other code wants to react.

vocabulary.def: Salesforce's pub/sub primitive. A custom event type you publish from Apex/Flow; subscribers across Salesforce or external systems handle it asynchronously.

slides.title: Async pitfalls
body: Silent failures — when async work fails, the originating save still succeeds (by design). Without explicit error logging, you'll never know. Common pattern: log every async failure to an Integration_Log__c custom object so failures are visible.

Race conditions — two parallel async paths can finish in any order. If path B assumes path A finished first, you'll get intermittent bugs. Use Queueable chaining (one job enqueues the next) when order matters.

slides.title: When to use which
body: @future: simple, primitive-only params, no chaining. Almost always use Queueable instead now.
Queueable: most new async work. Chainable. Complex types OK. Batchable: > a few thousand records. Schedulable: time-driven jobs.

slides.front: You need to send a Slack message after an Account is updated. Triggers can't make callouts. What's the pattern?
slides.back: From the trigger, enqueue a Queueable (or @future with callout=true). The async job makes the callout outside the trigger transaction.
slides.detail: Triggers must NOT make callouts directly. Pushing the work async fixes that AND insulates the user-facing save from external service hiccups.

slides.question: You need to recompute a stat on 5 million Opportunities every night. Which async type?
options: Batchable + Schedulable — chunked processing on a cron.
Synchronous — just write a regular method.
slides.explanation: Batchable processes large datasets in 200-row chunks (each with fresh limits); Schedulable wraps it with a cron. Together: nightly recompute, no governor blowup.

slides.title: Capstone: Queueable revenue updater
slides.intro: After a periodic price-list change, you need to bump every relevant Account's AnnualRevenue by 3%. Build a Queueable that does the SOQL + DML async — the standard pattern for any post-trigger background work in Salesforce.
lineExplanations: Class implements Queueable — that's the Apex contract for an async job.
Field to hold the work — the list of ids the job will process.
Constructor takes the ids the caller wants processed.
Store on the instance. 'this' disambiguates from the parameter.
Close the constructor.
execute() is what the platform calls when the job runs. Returns void; receives a context object.
Query the relevant Accounts in one SOQL call.
Get the field we'll modify.
From the Account object.
Bind the list of ids — single query for all of them.
Close the SOQL.
Walk the records.
Apply the 3% bump. Decimal arithmetic preserves precision.
Close the loop.
ONE bulk DML — never inside the loop. This is the bulkified pattern again.
Close execute.
Close the class.
Build a tiny test list of ids.
Enqueue the job. Returns a job Id; the work runs in the background.

slides.title: What you now know
body: @future, Queueable, Batchable, Schedulable — and when to choose each.
Next: the Capstone — bringing every concept from the core course onto a real consulting scenario (Arlington Property Management).
